Strip_Summary: The day you're born, they bring you home to the top floor of a tower in Manhattan. The entire floor is yours.
One day old — and you already have more space than most people ever will. There are men in dark suits outside the door.
You can hear a radio crackle through the wall sometimes. They always will be.
Your name shows up in newspapers before you can even say it. Cameras are already pointed at the building where you sl...
You did not choose this family. This family chose you.
You are four.
You speak two languages — your father's, and one from your mother's homeland, a small country in Eastern Europe that ...
You learn there are words you can say at home that you can't say at school. Not bad words. Just — different ones.
Your mother speaks to you in something that sounds like water. Soft consonants. Long vowels.
You answer in English. You're not sure when that started.
When you're seven, mornings have a pattern — you hear the engine outside before your cereal gets soggy. Black SUV. Sa...
You adjust your backpack straps. You don't look at it.
Your friends have to pass a background check before they can show up to your birthday. You find this out when your mo...
You discover video games around eight. You're not allowed online.
Your character runs through the same level for forty minutes because you don't know there's a next room.
You find it eventually. Nobody tells you. You figure it out. That part you like.
You peel the label off a bottle. Halfway. Then stop. Some parents say no. Not because of you — because of the paperwork.
The birthday chair is already a little too small for you.
At school, you use a different name. Something ordinary. Nobody knows which family you belong to.
A kid asks where you live. You say Midtown. He goes 'oh, cool' and turns back around. That's it.
One time a substitute teacher reads your real name off the list. The room gets weird for a second. Then it passes.
For a few hours, you're just a kid. That's it. You don't remember when that became normal.
The year before your father wins, you move three times. Three different bedrooms. Three different sets of neighbors. ...
Not because you stop liking things. Just — You know the drill.
You are ten. Your father wins. You move into a house with a hundred and thirty-two rooms.
Your footsteps echo. The hallways smell like old wood and something chemical — maybe paint, maybe polish. You're not ...
None of them feel like yours. You're the first son to live here since the sixties.
Your mother stays behind in New York a few more months — so you can finish the school year.
She chose you over the spotlight.
From here on. Everything changes. You think you understand how this works. You don't.
There are agents outside your classroom at the school in Maryland. Plain clothes. But everyone knows.
Your desk is the same as everyone else's. The hallway outside it isn't.
Once in a while you hear a walkie-talkie through the glass. A chair shifts in the hallway. Someone's always out there.
It was always like that. Lunch is the hardest part.
There are two agents at a table near the cafeteria entrance. Everyone else pretends not to look. You pretend not to n...
You can feel it — people looking, then looking away. You eat fast. You have gotten good at that.
You wipe your hands on a napkin. Then again.
A kid tries to sit with you once. You don't know if he's being nice or if his parents told him to.
You never figure it out.
Your friends want to come over. Federal security check first. Everyone knows your name. Nobody actually knows you.
Some stop asking. You try to explain it to someone once. It doesn't come out right.
You stop trying after that.
Other kids make plans after school. Yours need a security advance team.
A teacher keeps you after class. 'You doing alright?' You nod. That's the whole conversation.
Your siblings end up on cable news most nights, arguing about things you barely follow. You watch from the couch. Don...
Your sleeve catches on the armrest. You fix it without looking.
Your dad calls from downstairs. You can hear the TV still on. You pretend you're asleep.
Your brothers are older. You watch them argue on television and then see them at breakfast the next morning. They eat...
You don't understand how they do that. You ask your brother once. He looks at you. He says: You get used to it.
You think about that for a long time. Too long, probably. The thing is -
Nobody warns you about the quiet parts.
You get... quiet. Not on purpose. It just happens.
Your father gets impeached for the first time. You're thirteen. You find out the same way everyone else does. Phone. ...
The screen glows blue. Your blanket's pulled up to your chin. You lock your phone. Unlock it. Lock it again.
Nobody in the house explains it. You're old enough to read. You think about calling someone. You're not sure who.
The family leaves Washington when you're fourteen. The moving trucks show up again. Florida this time. You've lost co...
By seventeen, you're over six foot seven. Your phone is at eleven percent.
You can't disappear into a crowd anymore. Every room you walk into, you're the tallest person in it. You slouch at yo...
Your voice changed at fifteen. Nobody said anything. At sixteen, you walk into a hardware store with your security de...
The guy behind the counter looks past the agents. Looks at you. Says: Need help, sir?
Not because of who you are. Just because of how tall you are. You buy a lightbulb. That's it. The receipt is warm in ...
You don't say anything the whole drive home.
Your body made the decision your mind kept avoiding.
They photograph you walking. Just walking. That's it. That's the whole story.
Somehow you're eighteen. You're not sure when that happened. You graduate. Your father's running again.
They invite you to the convention. Delegate. You say no. No one asks what you want.
They announce prior commitments. The truth is simpler — you're not ready to be a symbol.
The night of the convention you watch it on your laptop. Alone.
The room is loud on screen. In your room it's just the fan. And the crowd through the speakers in a way that doesn't ...
Your name comes up twice. Both times you look away. You close the laptop at midnight. You don't remember falling asleep.
You turn it back on. Then off again. Inauguration. Second time. Cold. You're on the stage. Your breath fogs. Your fin...
You straighten your tie. Twice. You're taller than everyone up here. The cameras keep finding you. You didn't speak. ...
You just stood there. And that was more than enough.
There is a reception after. Someone hands you a drink. You hold it. People are talking to you. You nod.
You look for your mother in the crowd. She is there. You find her eventually.
She doesn't say anything. She just puts her hand on your arm. That's it.
The whole world is watching. And you haven't said a single word.
And then it's over. Just like that.
The fall after everything, you enroll in a business school in New York — Manhattan, the same city where you were born.
The subway rumbles under the building during lectures. You feel it in your chair. Same city. Same buildings. You're b...
Not just taller. First semester you eat alone a lot.
Not because you have to. Because the cafeteria is loud and you have never been good at walking into a room full of pe...
You find a corner table near the window. You can see the street from there. The table's sticky. You don't wipe it.
Taxis. People walking. Normal things. You eat and watch them. It feels normal. Or close to it.
Maybe ever. You don't question it.
The agents still follow you — into the lecture hall, outside your door at night.
You forget what you were about to say. It doesn't come back.
The other students figure it out by week two. Some are cool about it. One guy asks if they can take a photo.
You say sure. He looks nervous the whole time. His hands are shaking a little. Someone says your name wrong. You don'...
Your shoulders drop a little. But it does.
Behind you, the soft creak of a leather shoe shifting weight. Some things don't change.
But for the first time in your life, you actually chose something — the school, the city, even the major — and nobody...
Yeah. Business. Not law. Not politics. Not your father's path. You think — No. You chose this because you wanted to.
Or because it was the only thing left. You stop there. You don't think about it too long.
It is not freedom. But it is the closest you have ever had.
Sophomore year, you transfer to the campus in Washington. You move back into the old house. This time, you think it's...
You tell yourself you chose to come back. Most days, you believe it. You try to remember the first time. You can't.
Your father calls you the king of the internet. You laugh. Kind of.
You're the one who connected the campaign to a generation that doesn't watch television — podcasters, streamers, kids...
You understood them before anyone on the team did. Or at least... that's what people say.
You think you know what they respond to. Sincerity. Probably. At least the appearance of it. Which is the same thing ...
You're still not sure which side of that you're on. Sometimes you're talking to someone and you feel yourself being —...
Not just by the camera. By yourself.
You don't have a single social media account. Not one. But millions of people talk about you online every single day.
You hear someone say your name on the street. You don't turn around. Everyone's got an opinion.
Your clothes. Your face. Even your name - something about it. None of them asked.
People make accounts with your name — fake stories, edited photos, things you've never said attached to your face.
Your thumb hovers over the screen. You put the phone face-down.
You open a message. Don't read it. Close it. You pick it back up. Then put it down again. Nothing. You never respond.
Not once. You don't know if that's strength. Or something else. You wait.
People pitch you things in conference rooms — apps, drinks, buildings. Your phone buzzes once. You don't check it.
You tap the screen once. Just to see it light up. You're building something. Maybe.
Someone at a meeting calls you 'sir.' You almost laugh.
You attend the address to the nation. Front row. Someone hands you a glass of water. You don't drink it.
The seat is narrow. Your knees don't quite fit. You're taller than every legislator in the chamber.
There are events. Dinners. Things with names. The air feels thick. Too many people. You go to some of them.
You stand near the edge of the room because there's no other option. You're too visible anywhere.
Someone always comes up. Someone always has a story about knowing your father. You listen. You nod. You shake someone...
You check your watch. Then you remember you're not wearing one. You look at the window instead.
You still haven't spoken publicly. Not once. You almost — Nothing. You haven't said a word. Somehow that's what peopl...
You just sit with it. / You don't like thinking about any of this.
Remember the tower? The penthouse in Manhattan? The floor that was entirely yours?
The whole floor was yours. But you never really lived there.
You are twenty years old. And nobody knows what happens next. Not even you. And that's fine. Or it's not.
You leave it there. / And you still have not decided who you are.
But maybe — that is the one right no one can take from you.
